name: Overcoming the Monster
core_pattern: The protagonist sets out to defeat an evil, threatening force that endangers the protagonist's world
typical_arc: Threat emerges → hero is called → preparation → confrontation → monster defeated → world restored
tension_signature: Hero's strength vs. monster's power, order vs. chaos, courage vs. fear

name: Rags to Riches
core_pattern: The humble, overlooked protagonist acquires power, wealth, or a mate, loses it all, then gains it back through genuine growth
typical_arc: Initial deprivation → acquisition of gifts → initial success → crisis and loss → final redemption and true fulfillment
examples: Cinderella, David Copperfield, Jane Eyre, Aladdin
tension_signature: Worthiness vs. circumstance, inner virtue vs. outer status, authenticity vs. pretense

name: The Quest
core_pattern: The hero and companions journey to acquire an important object or reach a destination, overcoming temptations and obstacles
typical_arc: Call to quest → journey begins → trials and temptations → arrival at goal → goal achieved (often with sacrifice)
examples: The Odyssey, Lord of the Rings, Indiana Jones
tension_signature: Goal vs. obstacles, unity vs. division within the group, perseverance vs. despair

name: Voyage and Return
core_pattern: The hero travels to a strange, disorienting world, struggles to cope, then escapes back home, changed by the experience
typical_arc: Fall into strange world → initial wonder → shadow falls (threat or oppression) → thrilling escape → return home
examples: Alice in Wonderland, The Time Machine, Narnia, Gone with the Wind
tension_signature: Familiar vs. alien, belonging vs. exile, self vs. transformed self

name: Comedy
core_pattern: A series of confusions and misunderstandings create a dark, threatening world which is resolved through the revelation of truth
typical_arc: World of confusion → hero caught in web of misunderstanding → shadow of threat grows → everything comes to light → happy ending
examples: A Midsummer Night's Dream, Much Ado About Nothing, Four Weddings and a Funeral
tension_signature: Truth vs. illusion, freedom vs. constraint, individual vs. social expectation

name: Tragedy
core_pattern: A hero with great gifts becomes cut off from the world by an overriding obsession or fatal flaw, leading to catastrophic downfall
typical_arc: Hero's gifts shine → fatal flaw or dark obsession appears → increasingly isolated → catastrophic end
examples: Hamlet, Macbeth, Anna Karenina, Breaking Bad
tension_signature: Strength vs. flaw, aspiration vs. limitation, isolation vs. connection

name: Rebirth
core_pattern: The hero falls under a dark spell or curse, living in a kind of death until finally liberated by a redemptive act or figure
typical_arc: Hero under dark shadow → initial freedom lost → imprisonment (literal or spiritual) → redemptive act or figure appears → liberation and new life
examples: Sleeping Beauty, A Christmas Carol, The Secret Garden, Crime and Punishment
tension_signature: Light vs. darkness, redemption vs. damnation, stagnation vs. renewal
